title: Data Generation: Mechanisms and Examples
text: common settings — AFT PH baseline, random censoring, standard covariate distributions — rmst.sim() generates data one function call. Use covar_continuous(), covar_binary(), covar_categorical() define covariates directly. result data frame class rmst_simdata. Pass directly rmst.power() rmst.ss(): designs requiring explicit covariate-dependent censoring, stratified assignment, frailty, use full recipe system described .

title: Data Generation: Mechanisms and Examples
text: dependent block uses log-rate model: log⁡λc(X)=log⁡(b)+X⊤β, \log \lambda_c(X) = \log(b) + X^\top \beta,  b > 0 baseline rate. Implementation uses base rate, beta log-rate scale. base supplied, formula must include intercept. prefer log-intercept style, omit base include intercept formula beta, e.g. formula="~ 1 + age + sex"; beta=c(-3.5, 0.012, 0.35).
code: cz_dep <- list(   mode = "explicit",   dependent = list(     # sex is coded numeric 0/1 in these recipes; if yours is a factor, use I(as.numeric(sex)-1)     formula = "~ 0 + age + sex",   # NO intercept because we use 'base' as the rate     base    = 0.03,                # positive baseline rate     beta    = c(0.012, 0.35)       # (age, sex) on log-rate scale   ),   administrative = list(time = 48) # optional admin cutoff )

what: Using a censoring recipe inside a full simulation
title: Data Generation: Mechanisms and Examples
text: Achieved censoring rate First 8 rows simulated data
code: # Covariates (sex will be numeric 0/1 here) covs <- list(   list(name="age", type="continuous",  dist="normal",    params=list(mean=62, sd=10)),   list(name="sex", type="categorical", dist="bernoulli", params=list(p=0.45)) )  # Censoring: single covariate-dependent mechanism using a positive base rate cz_dep <- list(   mode = "explicit",   dependent = list(     formula = "~ 0 + age + sex",  # NO intercept; sex is numeric 0/1     base    = 0.03,               # baseline censoring rate (>0)     beta    = c(0.012, 0.35)      # (age, sex) on log-rate scale   ),   administrative = list(time = 48) )  # Quick sanity check (optional) mm   <- model.matrix(~ 0 + age + sex, data.frame(age=c(50,70), sex=c(0,1))) rate <- cz_dep$dependent$base * exp(mm %*% matrix(cz_dep$dependent$beta, ncol=1)) stopifnot(all(rate > 0 & is.finite(rate)))  # Recipe rec <- list(   n = 300,   covariates = list(defs = covs),   treatment  = list(assignment="randomization", allocation="1:1"),   event_time = list(     model    = "ph_pwexp",     baseline = list(rates = c(0.05), cuts = numeric(0)), # exponential baseline     effects  = list(intercept=0, treatment=-0.3, covariates=list(age=0.01, sex=-0.2))   ),   censoring  = cz_dep,   seed = 2025 )  # Simulate dat <- simulate_from_recipe(validate_recipe(rec))  # Output knitr::kable(   data.frame(achieved_censoring = attr(dat, "achieved_censoring")),   caption = "Achieved censoring rate" ) knitr::kable(utils::head(dat, 8), caption = "First 8 rows of simulated data")

title: Data Generation: Mechanisms and Examples
text: example uses ~ formulas event-time covariate effects covariate-dependent censoring. keep treatment effect separate scalar avoid design ambiguity. Achieved censoring rate (formula-based example) First 8 rows — formula-based example
code: # Covariates (sex numeric 0/1) covs_f <- list(   list(name="age", type="continuous",  dist="normal",    params=list(mean=62, sd=10)),   list(name="sex", type="categorical", dist="bernoulli", params=list(p=0.45)) )  trt_f <- list(assignment="randomization", allocation="1:1")  # Event-time engine: AFT lognormal with formula-based covariate effects evt_f <- list(   model    = "aft_lognormal",   baseline = list(mu = 3.1, sigma = 0.55),   effects  = list(     treatment  = -0.25,                 # effect of treatment (log-time scale)     formula    = "~ 0 + age + sex",     # covariate effects via formula (no intercept)     beta       = c(0.010, -0.20)        # (age, sex) -- match order in formula   ) )  # Censoring: covariate-dependent via log-rate model (single mechanism) cz_f <- list(   mode = "explicit",   dependent = list(     formula = "~ 0 + age + sex",  # NO intercept; sex numeric 0/1     base    = 0.03,               # positive rate     beta    = c(0.012, 0.35)      # (age, sex) on log-rate scale   ),   administrative = list(time = 48)  # optional admin cutoff )  # Full recipe rec_f <- list(   n = 350,   covariates = list(defs = covs_f),   treatment  = trt_f,   event_time = evt_f,   censoring  = cz_f,   seed = 777 )  # Simulate dat_f <- simulate_from_recipe(validate_recipe(rec_f))  # Display knitr::kable(   data.frame(achieved_censoring = attr(dat_f, "achieved_censoring")),   caption = "Achieved censoring rate (formula-based example)" ) knitr::kable(utils::head(dat_f, 8), caption = "First 8 rows — formula-based example")

title: RMSTpowerBoost: Sample Size and Power Calculations for RMST-based Clinical Trials
text: Clinical trials time--event endpoints often rely hazard-based methods proportional hazards (PH) model hazard ratio (HR). HR can hard interpret treatment effects vary time, proportional hazards assumption often doubtful practice. Restricted Mean Survival Time (RMST) expected event-free time pre-specified follow-point, LL(Royston Parmar 2013; Uno et al. 2014). yields treatment contrast time scale, average gain several months clinically relevant horizon. Recent work models RMST directly function baseline covariates instead estimating survival curve hazard model. Methods based Inverse Probability Censoring Weighting (IPCW) (Tian et al. 2014) now cover stratified studies (Wang et al. 2019; Zhang Schaubel 2024) covariate-dependent censoring (Wang Schaubel 2018). available software emphasizes estimation existing data rather study design. result, trial statisticians often need custom code sample size power calculations. RMSTpowerBoost implements direct RMST methods power sample size calculations: Linear IPCW Models: direct regression model RMST IPCW-based estimation (Tian et al. 2014). Stratified Models: Efficient methods studies large number strata (e.g., clinical centers), including additive (Zhang Schaubel 2024) multiplicative (Wang et al. 2019) models. Dependent Censoring Models: Methods handling covariate-dependent censoring (Wang Schaubel 2018). Flexible Non-Linear Models: Bootstrap-based functions using Generalized Additive Models (GAMs) capture non-linear covariate effects. Analytic vs. Bootstrap Methods: models, package offers choice fast analytical calculation simulation-based boot method. vignette outlines main model families shows use .

title: RMSTpowerBoost: Sample Size and Power Calculations for RMST-based Clinical Trials

title: Analyze Power for a Stratified Additive RMST Model (Analytic) — additive.power.analytical
text: function computes power semiparametric additive RMST model \(\mu_{ij} = \mu_{0j} + \beta'Z_i\), indexes subjects j indexes strata. method uses Inverse Probability Censoring Weighting (IPCW), weights derived stratified Cox model censoring times. regression coefficient \(\hat{\beta}\) estimated centering covariates RMST values within stratum solving resulting estimating equations closed form. Power obtained asymptotic sandwich variance \(\hat{\beta}\). implementation uses robust variance estimator \(A_n^{-1} B_n (A_n^{-1})'\), \(A_n\) \(B_n\) empirical estimates variance components.

title: Analyze Power for a Stratified Additive RMST Model (Analytic) — additive.power.analytical
code: set.seed(123) pilot_df_strat <- data.frame(  time = rexp(150, 0.1),  status = rbinom(150, 1, 0.8),  arm = rep(0:1, each = 75),  region = factor(rep(c("A", "B", "C"), each = 50)),  age = rnorm(150, 60, 10) ) # Introduce an additive treatment effect pilot_df_strat$time[pilot_df_strat$arm == 1] <-   pilot_df_strat$time[pilot_df_strat$arm == 1] + 1.5  power_results <- additive.power.analytical(   pilot_data = pilot_df_strat,   time_var = "time", status_var = "status", arm_var = "arm", strata_var = "region",   sample_sizes = c(100, 150, 200),   linear_terms = "age",   L = 12 ) #> --- Estimating parameters from pilot data... --- #> --- Estimating additive effect via stratum-centering... --- #> --- Calculating asymptotic variance... --- #> --- Calculating power for specified sample sizes... --- print(power_results$results_data) #>   N_per_Stratum     Power #> 1           100 0.2629389 #> 2           150 0.3682932 #> 3           200 0.4660482 print(power_results$results_plot)

title: Find Sample Size for a Stratified Additive RMST Model (Analytic) — additive.ss.analytical
code: set.seed(123) pilot_df_strat <- data.frame(  time = rexp(150, 0.1),  status = rbinom(150, 1, 0.8),  arm = rep(0:1, each = 75),  region = factor(rep(c("A", "B", "C"), each = 50)),  age = rnorm(150, 60, 10) ) # Introduce an additive treatment effect pilot_df_strat$time[pilot_df_strat$arm == 1] <-   pilot_df_strat$time[pilot_df_strat$arm == 1] + 1.5    # Find the required sample size per stratum for 80% power   ss_results <- additive.ss.analytical(     pilot_data = pilot_df_strat,     time_var = "time", status_var = "status",     arm_var = "arm", strata_var = "region",     target_power = 0.50,     L = 18, #     n_start = 200,     n_step = 50,     max_n_per_arm = 1000   ) #> --- Estimating parameters from pilot data for analytic search... --- #> --- Searching for Sample Size (Method: Additive Analytic) --- #>   N = 200/stratum, Calculated Power = 0.399 #>   N = 250/stratum, Calculated Power = 0.478 #>   N = 300/stratum, Calculated Power = 0.55 #>  #> --- Calculation Summary --- #>  #>  #> Table: Required Sample Size #>  #> | Target_Power| Required_N_per_Stratum| #> |------------:|----------------------:| #> |          0.5|                    300|   print(ss_results$results_data) #>   Target_Power Required_N_per_Stratum #> 1          0.5                    300   print(ss_results$results_plot)

title: Analyze Power for RMST Model with Covariate-Dependent Censoring (Analytic) — DC.power.analytical
text: function assumes single censoring process whose hazard depends covariates (treatment default). fits Cox model censoring $$\Pr(\text{censoring } t \mid X) = 1 - G(t \mid X)$$ using Surv(time, status==0) ~ linear\_terms, forms inverse-probability--censoring weights (IPCW) \(w_i = 1/\hat G(Y_i\mid X_i)\) evaluated \(Y_i=\min(T_i,L)\). RMST regression \(E[Y_i \mid A_i,X_i]\) fit weighted least squares, power derived sandwich variance ignores uncertainty estimating \(\hat G\). Note: implementation models single censoring process handle competing risks.

title: Calculate Power for a Semiparametric Additive RMST Model via Simulation — GAM.power.boot
code: pilot_df <- data.frame(   time = rexp(100, 0.08),   status = rbinom(100, 1, 0.7),   arm = rep(0:1, each = 50),   age = rnorm(100, 60, 10) ) # Add a treatment effect pilot_df$time[pilot_df$arm == 1] <- pilot_df$time[pilot_df$arm == 1] * 1.3  power_results <- GAM.power.boot(   pilot_data = pilot_df,   time_var = "time",   status_var = "status",   arm_var = "arm",   sample_sizes = c(100, 150),   linear_terms = "age",   L = 15,   n_sim = 100, # Use more sims in practice, e.g., 1000   parallel.cores = 2 ) #> --- Calculating Power (Method: Additive GAM for RMST) --- #> Model: pseudo_obs ~ arm + age #> Simulating for n = 100 /arm ... #> Simulating for n = 150 /arm ... #> Total simulation time: 57.21 seconds #>  #> --- Simulation Summary --- #>  #>  #> Table: Estimated Treatment Effect (RMST Difference) #>  #> |Statistic            |      Value| #> |:--------------------|----------:| #> |Mean RMST Difference |  0.2837699| #> |Mean Standard Error  |  0.6255794| #> |95% CI Lower         | -1.0747207| #> |95% CI Upper         |  1.6422605| print(power_results$results_data) #>   N_per_Group Power #> 1         100  0.08 #> 2         150  0.08 print(power_results$results_plot)

title: Analyze Power for a Linear RMST Model (Analytic) — linear.power.analytical
text: function implements analytic power calculation direct linear regression model Restricted Mean Survival Time (RMST) proposed Tian et al. (2014). core method weighted linear model form $$E[Y_i \mid A_i, \mathbf{Z}_i] = \alpha + \tau A_i + \mathbf{Z}_i^\top \boldsymbol{\gamma}$$ \(Y_i = \min(T_i, L)\) event time truncated \(L\), \(A_i\) treatment indicator, \(\tau\) treatment effect interest. handle right-censoring, method uses Inverse Probability Censoring Weighting (IPCW). weight uncensored individual inverse probability remaining uncensored event time, \(w_i = \delta_i / \hat{G}(Y_i)\), \(\hat{G}(t) = P(C > t)\) Kaplan-Meier estimate censoring distribution. Power calculated analytically based asymptotic properties coefficient estimators. variance treatment effect estimator, \(\hat{\tau}\), derived robust sandwich variance estimator form \(^{-1}B(^{-1})'\). implementation, scaled information matrix \((X'WX)/n\), B empirical second moment influence functions, \((\sum \epsilon_i \epsilon_i')/n\), \(\epsilon_i\) influence curve observation . resulting variance used calculate standard error given sample size, turn used power formula.

title: Analyze Power for a Multiplicative Stratified RMST Model (Analytic) — MS.power.analytical
text: function based method evaluating center (stratum) effects using multiplicative model RMST: \(\mu_{ij} = \mu_{0j} \exp\{\beta'Z_i\}\). method uses IPCW stratified Cox model censoring distribution. Formal estimation \(\beta\) requires iterative solver estimating equation given Equation (8) Wang et al. (2019). computationally intensive, implementation uses log-linear working model fitted weighted least squares pseudo-observations (lm(log(Y_rmst) ~ ...)) tractable approximation. approximation consistent log-linear mean structure well-specified may differ formal estimator strong misspecification. power calculation relies asymptotic variance log-RMST ratio estimator, \(\hat{\tau}\). variance derived robust variance-covariance matrix lm fit, serves proxy formal sandwich estimator \(^{-1}B(^{-1})'\) described Theorem 1 Wang et al. (2019).

Default 1000. parallel.cores Number cores parallel processing. Default 1.
